Hey guys, welcome to the final installment of Seven Figure Emails. This is number seven, the sarcastic Sally, otherwise known as the Deadpool. If this is the first one you're watching, for whatever reason, maybe like starting backwards, dessert first, appetizers last, something like that. This is how it goes down. We're going to start with the background on the template, how it came to be, why it's even a thing. Then we'll do the persuasion deep dive, what's really going on psychologically, what are the persuasion triggers that play that make this work so well. Then we're going to look at two versions of the template and in the video below, this one we will break down two examples so you could really see how it played out in a real live launch. So a little bit of the background, we rarely, rarely, rarely, rarely, rarely, if ever reuse email copy across multiple launches or funnels, but there was one closing email that worked so well in our Tenix Emails Mastery launch in 2017 that we had to use it again in 2018. And both those launches were super successful. They both cleared six figures in a short four-day launch window without any crazy webinars or ads behind it. So it was really an email-driven launch. This one was the only email to appear in two launches, I think, ever. So it was one of our more successful ones. And once we saw that it was playing so well and performing so well, we really decided to see what's at play here and break it down and formulate it so that you can do the same. So at its core, this template leverages sarcasm as a framing device, as a pattern interrupt for the sales arguments that may not have landed with enough impact earlier in your launch. And by repackaging your testimonials, your benefits, your big promise, your objection handling within the frame of sarcasm, what we're trying to do here is just cut through the white noise and make sure those key elements, those core elements that will coach the conversion and lead to the sale, are read and absorbed with full attentiveness. So while some people consider sarcasm to be the lowest form of wit, most people still dig it and more importantly, get it. You know, this Chandler Bing sarcasm king season on YouTube. There's a whole slew of videos dedicated just to Chandler Bing montages of him and his sarcasm. Now, I was never a big fan hated friends. Sorry, don't hate me for saying that. But there's no denying how even like 15 years after it's been off the air, Chandler Bing is still such a recognizable character. And it's all rooted in his sarcastic remarks and his sarcastic replies. So there's still a lot of people that resonate with Chandler Bing's sarcasm. A more recent example, Deadpool grossed over $1.5 billion worldwide. And I think there's only the first one, the second one may have done even more than that. And he is kind of like, this was kind of an unexpected hit in Hollywood, they weren't expecting this to be as big as it was. His humor is all sarcastic driven sarcasm driven. And there were multiple branded campaigns and commercials that all leveraged that same style. So sarcasm, love it or hate it, it is still highly influential and it still works really well, mostly because people get it. It's not too high brow, it's not too low brow, it's kind of right there in between. So the persuasion deep dive, what's actually working in this template, how do we make it practical? Well, the first one is the good old pattern interrupt. The main reason this email works is because it cuts through the white noise and really delivers those critical sales arguments within a new and entertaining frame, you know, you're being sarcastic from the beginning, right up until almost the end. So you're in that frame, it's entertaining, it's different. And therefore, all the arguments that may have been ignored before, not seen or not paid attention to are all of a sudden given greater attentiveness. It's about getting those arguments seen felt absorbed more deeply by using a frame that commands commands attention. Next thing is preempting objections, aka objection, Ikea, so almost every product or service will suffer from some industry wide objection. For example, nobody finishes online courses, realtors aren't worth their cut. The keto diet doesn't work, going full vegan doesn't work, email is dead, all these things are those big industry wide objections that you can overcome with sarcasm. So by getting ahead of the objection and using its own momentum to your benefit, you perform what we call objection Ikea that not only disarms it, but strengthens your position because you're displaying such confidence by even using that sarcastic tone by saying, yeah, email is dead, and then you get into kind of that sarcastic frame. So that's what's going on here with sarcasm, persuasion, principle number three, it playfully positions you against the alternative, not only can you preempt objections, you can also use that sarcastic framing to position against pretty much anything including different product categories or alternatives, or even your prospects, status grower and nurture or inaction, you could playfully position against just about anything using sarcasm, whereas it might be more difficult to call those things out in a more direct or literal way. Some very important writing tips, if you are going to use sarcasm, especially with the sarcastic sally, maintain that sarcastic frame right up until the end or at least don't shift back and forth between sarcasm and being literal, because if you cherry pick where you use it, it becomes kind of confusing and harder to decipher where you're being sarcastic and when you're being sincere. Next thing when you are being sarcastic, especially in self deprecating, especially in self deprecating, you are your product or lifting up alternatives, err on the side of exaggeration, be a bit more absurd so that your reader is absolutely clear that you're being sarcastic so that there's no room for confusion on that. And then finally, the opening should be self deprecating, make fun of yourself or your product category, if you're a realtor or whatever it is, start off by being self deprecating, which gives you permission later on to be a little more down or self or deprecating against your competitors or other alternatives later. So come out right out of the gates using sarcasm against yourself so that it doesn't come off as a mean spirit and like you're just attacking other people in a sarcastic way. Alright, so the template itself, two versions, the first one, you can start off with the sarcastic self deprecating opener, so this is really making fun of yourself in a sarcastic way like email is dead, realtors suck, whatever it is, just try to make that deprecating against you or the industry or space you work in, state the so-called alternatives, what's the alternative to that? Maintaining that sarcastic frame, give that moment of highest pleasure so the benefits are going to achieve in a very vivid three-dimensional way, but frame it as a negative and then contrast with how the alternative would look framed as a positive. So this is where you might look at the moment of highest tension but framed as a positive instead, testimonials, so what other people experience in your product, write those sarcastically like they only did this, you know, that sucks, we feel bad for this person and then break the frame by listing the big benefits and promise. So really only break the frame at the end of this and then you can have a play it safe PS to argue and gain so that misconception do you open with, say like of course realtors don't suck, you know, of course email is in dead, so that's your play it safe PS, but as long as you're being a little bit airing on the side of the absurd people will know your sarcastic anyway, it's very hard to get this wrong as long as you maintain that frame throughout and pay attention to these VIP writing tips over here. So, theme plate number two, number B, you'll see an example of this as well. So we'll start off right with a battlefield, drop in right in. Do you want to experience a certain moment of highest tension? Well, that's easy and then simply give the path towards that moment of highest tension and then break frame, break sarcastically, sarcastically frame that moment of highest tension itself as being desirable like yeah mediocre career is awesome and say why it is and then break frame transitioning to those who want to escape it through your product and exclusive empowerment is what empowers them to be able to take action on that call to action and a final close that provides contrast to your opening and optional bullets to support your webinar or product. So we're going to see what this actually looks like in play in the video below for both templates. So give that a look and I will catch you in that next video.
